Welcome to the Club
Social networks' growth patterns can vary as widely as their logos and user interfaces. Here's a look at what buckets some notable social sites fall into.

The 100 Million User Club
Facebook: The social giant has tallied an unprecedented 1 billion users.
Twitter: Boasts a half billion total users, including approximately 170 million actively Tweeting.
Google+: With its built-in Google+ base, it's an instant power player on any year after its launch.
Weibo: Buoyed by a lack of competition, the powerhouse Chinese microblogging site has grown at a startling rate.
RenRen: Questions about how this Chinese social network's users are active.
LinkedIn: Has defined typical social growth patterns — it's the only member of the “Century Club” that has reached a similar milestone.
Badoo: Most popular in parts of Europe and Latin America; it's quietly built a formidable user base.
Instagram: The newest addition to the club took less than two years to cross the 100 million mark.

The Rising Stars
Foursquare: As smartphones have exploded, so has this location-based social site, more than doubling its user base in the last year.
Instagram: This photo-sharing site has continued to grow rapidly.
Tumblr: The microblogging platform has doubled its user base in the last year.
Flickr: The photo sharing site continues to grow.
Path: The mobile-only photo-sharing site grew nicely.
SoundCloud: This collaborative audio distribution platform has built a solid base.

The Steady Freddies
The Child Prodigies
Pinterest: This image-based discovery engine has boomed in the last several years.

Cooling Off
Friendster: Reached 200 million users; its rebound has slowed.
MySpace: Slowed dramatically after its peak.

Sources and Methodology
Silverpop consulted more than 85 sources to gain as accurate a picture as possible of social network growth during the past decade. In addition to official corporate pages for Badoo, Facebook, Flickr, LinkedIn, and Twitter, data sources included:

Mashable.com

HOW TO CREATE

CALLIGRAPHY

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your left elbow next to your paper! if you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape
hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen
keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot
you can use your brush to transfer a small amount of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib
you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!
make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch
cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

(adgh example lettering)

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line below the bottom of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form a guide for measuring the size and height of your calligraphy letters. Hold your pen (or pencil) vertical to the paper and make a mark for the top of your nib. Repeat this down the page to create a ladder of marks. Draw straight lines across to create a set of regular nib heights.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical nibbed lowercase is about 4.5 nib heights tall. Using your nib ladder, measure up 4.5 nib heights from the baseline and draw the x-height. This is the main height of most lowercase letters.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure a further 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw the ascender and descender lines. These mark how high ascenders (b, d, h) should rise and how low descenders (g, q, y) should fall.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. Use your nib measurements to establish the cap height for your capitals.

WARNING
If you're gripping your nib holder too tightly or drawing at the wrong angle your nib will catch on the paper and you'll get blots. Before you begin try some practice strokes on scrap paper. We recommend using a chisel-tip marker first to practise shapes and control. Work slowly and don't press too hard with your nib.

SPRING FESTIVAL & LUNAR NEW YEAR.

The date changes every year because it is based on the lunar-solar Chinese calendar.

The festival happens in winter, when people look forward to the renewal of spring.

THE CHINESE NEW YEAR,
春节, CHUN JIE or "CHUN-JYEAH"
has been observed for more than 3,000 years.

THE

People observing Chinese New Year often celebrate with special decorations, fireworks and firecrackers, and red envelopes filled with money.

CHINESE NEW YEAR
DECORATIONS
are often red because red is considered lucky in the Chinese culture.

CHINESE NEW YEAR FOODS
are often deemed lucky based on symbolic meanings and/or if the name of a food sounds similar to other Chinese words for luck or wealth.

6 LUCKY
CHINESE NEW YEAR
FOODS

1
CITRUS FRUIT
- In Chinese the word for pomelo sounds like the word for "to have."
- Tangerines, mandarins, and kumquats are said to evoke gold, so having them can bring wealth and increase prosperity.
- The word for orange or tangerine sounds like the Chinese word for success.
- Another way to write tangerine is comprised of the Chinese character for luck.

2
DUMPLINGS
- Chinese dumplings symbolize great wealth.
- Depending on how they are prepared, dumplings can be made to resemble silver ingots or gold bars.

3
SPRING ROLLS OR EGG ROLLS
- Similar to dumplings, egg rolls and spring rolls may resemble gold bars.
- Chinese New Year spring rolls symbolize wealth.

4
LONGEVITY NOODLES
- The length of the noodles represents long life.
- Cutting the noodles into small pieces is frowned upon, as it means the person's life is to be cut short.

5
RICE CAKE
- In Chinese legend, rice cakes would be prepared and served to the gods as offerings during New Year's, in the hopes that when the gods received the rice cakes they would grant a prosperous year.
- The Chinese words for these cakes sound like the words for "year" and "higher," so eating them is said to bring a higher year and greater success.
- The sweetness of the cakes also represents a sweet life.

6
WHOLE FISH
- Serving whole fish symbolizes prosperity.
- The Chinese word for fish sounds like the word for surplus, so leaving some of the fish uneaten traditionally ensures there will be abundance in the year to come.

CHINESE
NEW YEAR
DUMPLING LORE

Flat dumplings symbolize poverty, so New Year dumplings are often shaped with many pleats.

If you eat a dumpling that contains a white thread, you'll have long life.

A dumpling with a coin inside means you'll get rich.

When serving, dumplings should be arranged in lines, not circles, because a circle suggests that you won't make progress in life and will keep going in circles.

To pass on wishes of amazing wealth, when eating dumplings in this traditional way, we say:

"ZHĀO CÁI JÌN BĀO"
JAOW TSEYE JIN BAOWW
招财进宝
meaning "Bring in wealth and treasure!"

HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink

gouache paint
paper

tissues
a couple of pencils

water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach
tape your paper to the desk for extra stability
work on a slanted desk or a table if possible
give yourself plenty of room to work
don't keep your ink right next to your paper - if you knock it over, you'll spoil your work
make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

use your brush to transfer a small amount of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

(adgh sample word image)

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line at the bottom of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to form consistently-sized letters. Using your set square, draw a series of parallel horizontal lines spaced one nib-width apart. These lines show the height of each nib of your pen. Continue the ladder until you have formed a diagonal line of 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib widths tall. Using your set square, measure 4-5 nib widths up from the baseline and draw a horizontal line across the page. This is the x-height.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below and draw two horizontal lines to mark the ascender and descender. The ascender is the top of tall lowercase letters and the descender is the bottom of letters that drop below the baseline.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It sits above the x-height and is usually equal to the ascender height.

WARNING
If you're drawing your nib ladder with ink, your paper may curl slightly while it dries. To avoid smudging, allow plenty of drying time before drawing the ascender and descender lines. We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker when marking up your paper - it gives good coverage and dries quickly.

BUSINESS ANALYTICS vs DATA SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation. To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

Decision-Making
- Instigate change by transforming business insights into tangible resources for decision-making.

Applied Problem Solving
- Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into actionable steps that can be solved.

Data Analysis
- Organize, interpret, and visualize data using predictive, prescriptive, and descriptive analytics to make it useful and accessible for business users.

Analytical Models
- Develop and apply statistical models using tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and KNIME.

question: What is the percentage of total units that CBR released for regenerative medicine applications in the last 8 years?
prediction_claude_sonnet__claude_sonnet: According to the document, 60% of total cord blood units were released for regenerative medicine applications within the last 8 years. This information is listed under the "Fueling Awareness, Advancing Science" section.
extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
Healthy Futures - 20 Years and Counting

The year was 1992. Disney's Aladdin was the top movie of the year. Space Shuttle Endeavor completed its maiden voyage. In a lab at the University of Arizona, CBR was born - and with it, the entire family cord blood banking industry.

A History of Firsts
1992: The 1st family cord blood sample is stored at CBR's Scientific Director's Laboratory at University of Arizona
1993: CBR Scientific Director releases first family banked sample for transplant
1996: CBR launches the first program to provide free banking services to families with medical need
1998: CBR is the 1st cord blood bank accredited by the American Association of Blood Banks
2004: CBR releases the 1st unit in the world to be used by a child for a brain injury
2005: CBR builds a new state-of-the-art lab in Tucson
2010: CBR is the 1st to offer cord tissue banking to families in the U.S.
2010: CBR collaborates with Georgia Health Sciences University to initiate the first FDA-regulated clinical trial for cerebral palsy using a child's own cord blood stem cells
2012: 425,000 samples banked - and counting

CBR Today
We're the largest, safest located, most experienced stem cell bank in the world — and we've got the numbers to prove it.
- 425,000+ Newborn stem cell samples banked, more than any other family bank in the world
- 38,569 Healthcare providers around the world who have collected samples for CBR
- 3,744 Hospitals that have used CBR's collection kit, including 753 international hospitals
- 100+ Countries store cord blood in CBR's bank
- 50 States are represented in CBR's bank

Fueling Awareness, Advancing Science
As newborn stem cell science grows, CBR is at the forefront of the industry.
- 25,000 Inquiries worldwide about potential use of stem cells in the last 20 years
- 200+ Clinical trials underway worldwide evaluating cord blood stem cells, including 4 CBR-exclusive trials
- 60 Percentage of total cord blood released for regenerative medicine applications, all within the last 8 years
- 4,000+ Samples CBR has banked free of charge, thanks to their Newborn Possibilities Program
- 3,000 Number of physicians who have collected samples in support of CBR's Newborn Possibilities Program

The City Inside CBR
It may be cold and dark inside CBR's stem cell storage tanks called Dewars, but there's a population of cord blood units safely tucked away — and thriving — equal in size to a large city.
- -196°C Internal temperature of the Dewars
- Less than 24 hours Average time from sample arrival at the lab to freeze

Dewars' population:
- 10,465 sets of twins
- 308 sets of triplets
- 12 sets of quads
- 1 set of quints

Some Things Don't Change
Number of chances to collect and store cord blood stem cells for your baby: 1

Sources: *Butler MG, Menitove JE. Umbilical cord blood banking: an update. J Assist Reprod Genet. 2011;28(8):669-76.
**U.S. National Institutes of Health clinicaltrials.gov

Cord Blood Registry® Healthy Futures Born Here

question: In which year first national bank established?

extracted_text_gpt-5-mini__gpt-5-mini:
A HISTORY OF
AMERICAN PAPER MONEY

FROM COLONIAL BILLS TO SILVER AND GOLD CERTIFICATES TO EVEN A $100,000 NOTE, AMERICAN PAPER MONEY HAS UNDERGONE MANY CHANGES THROUGHOUT ITS HISTORY.

1781
FIRST NATIONAL BANK
The Congress charters the Bank of North America. It becomes the first national bank.

1775
CONTINENTAL CONGRESS
Paper money is issued by the Continental Congress to finance the Revolutionary War.

COLONIAL BILLS
The Massachusetts Bay Colony issues the first paper currency in the American colonies to pay for expenses from colonial wars.

1785
THE U.S. DOLLAR
Congress adopts the dollar as the monetary unit of the United States.

1791
FIRST U.S. CENTRAL BANK
Congress charters the First Bank of the United States as the national bank.

1792
FEDERAL MONETARY SYSTEM
Coinage Act establishes the U.S. dollar and creates the U.S. Mint.

1816
SECOND U.S. CENTRAL BANK
Congress charters the Second Bank of the United States for the years 1816-1836.

1836
FREE BANKING ERA
Free banking era begins; state-chartered banks issue banknotes that vary widely in value and trustworthiness.

1861
CIVIL WAR "GREENBACKS"
Congress authorizes the issuance of demand notes, also called "greenbacks" to finance the Civil War. These are the first paper currency issued by the federal government for circulation.

1862
FIRST $2, $5, $20, AND $100 BILLS
Legal tender notes begin to be issued in larger denominations to finance the Civil War and government operations.

1865
ESTABLISHMENT OF U.S. SECRET SERVICE
The U.S. Secret Service is established as an agency to combat counterfeiting and to protect the national currency.

1877
BUREAU OF ENGRAVING AND PRINTING
The Treasury Department creates the Bureau of Engraving and Printing to standardize the production of paper currency.

1878
FIRST SILVER CERTIFICATES
Silver certificates start being issued, redeemable in silver dollars.

1882
FIRST GOLD CERTIFICATES
Gold certificates are issued; these notes represent gold coin and were used for large-value transfers.

1913
FEDERAL RESERVE ACT OF 1913
The Federal Reserve System is created; Federal Reserve Notes become the primary U.S. paper currency.

1914
LARGE-SIZE FEDERAL RESERVE NOTES
$1, $2, $5, $10, $20, $50 and $100 large-size Federal Reserve Notes are issued.

1918
LARGE SIZE HIGH DENOMINATIONS
New high-denomination notes are issued: $500, $1,000, $5,000, $10,000 and $100,000 notes are used primarily for interbank transactions and government use.

1928
STANDARDIZED DESIGN
To lower production costs and simplify banking, notes are standardized in design and size. Portraits of presidents and famous Americans appear on all denominations; reverse designs and seal placements become uniform across notes.

1934
$100,000 GOLD CERTIFICATES
The highest denomination ever printed, these were used only for transactions between Federal Reserve Banks and were not circulated publicly.

1957
"IN GOD WE TRUST"
$1 Silver certificates were the first notes to have the motto "In God We Trust."

1990
NEW COUNTERFEIT DETERRENT METHODS
Microprinting and security threads are introduced to reduce counterfeiting and improve banknote security.

1996-2000
REDESIGN OF PAPER MONEY
Multicolored backgrounds, enlarged portraits, watermark technology, and security threads become standard. New designs and enhanced security features are gradually introduced beginning in 1996 and continued through 2000, on small-size notes since 1929.

2003-2006
UPDATED SECURITY FEATURES
Updated security features are introduced, including enhanced portraits, watermarks, color-shifting ink, and improved security threads. These measures are part of the first redesigns for some denominations in decades.

2007
NEW $5 BILL WITH AN ALL DIGITAL DESIGN
This note features an updated, modern look to make counterfeiting more difficult and to improve durability.

2010
NEW $100 BILL DESIGN
The new $100 bill is released, featuring advanced anti-counterfeiting features including 3-D security ribbon and enhanced color-shifting devices.

References
www.federalreserve.gov/pubs/bulletin/old/2012/foreword.htm
www.frbsf.org/currency/about/notes/history.html
www.usmoneyhistory.com/Content/us_currency_history.html

BREAK THE CYCLE:
STEPS TO PROTECT YOUR FAMILY AGAINST VIOLENCE

THE CYCLE OF DOMESTIC ABUSE

Developed in the 1970s, The Cycle of Abuse by Lenore Walker explores the stages of an abusive relationship:

TENSION

Tension lays the foundation for domestic violence. The abuser - a husband, father or boyfriend - begins to exhibit passive-aggressive behaviour toward his victim - a wife, mother or girlfriend. The relationship becomes strained and tension builds as the behaviour worsens. Victims here notoriously try to change their behaviour to soften the intensity of their partner's abuse.

INCIDENT

But as it nearly always turns out - no level of obedience on the part of the victim will stop their aggressor's rage. Passive-aggressive behaviour eventually erupts in an overt or covert act of abuse - from physical abuse; hitting, kicking, shoving or throwing objects, to tell-tale control behaviours; such as emotional abuse, neglect or intimidation.

RECONCILIATION

After the act of abuse is over, the victim is left shaken, questioning how she landed in this situation. At this point, the abuser starts to backtrack on his actions - and is quick to repent, resolve, and reassure his bruised victim that he'll change - that this will never happen again. He tries to provide a blanket solution to the incident - buying presents or threatening self-harm - if she leaves.

CALM

The calm stage - much like the calm before the storm - is thought to extend the reconciliation period as relative peace between the two ensues. The victim is now in the thick of a very destructive situation - blind sighted to their partner's pleas he has changed. But the calm only lasts so long as the storm begins to brew.

THE CYCLE ENDS WITH YOU

The road to end domestic violence is not impossible. Follow these practical steps to reverse the damage before it takes hold:

Tell someone about your experience
Reach out for help and don't go it alone - speak to family or friends you trust about your situation.

Safely challenge the behaviour
If you witness actual physical violence against a woman, call the police on 131 444 or 000 in the case of emergency.

Call the NSW Domestic Violence Line
For direct advice on how you can contact a reliable and qualified domestic violence advisor on 1800 656 463.

Consider the Aurora App
If you are experiencing domestic violence right now, or are worried you might be entering the cycle, download the Aurora App (free) for domestic violence risk support and services.

Lead by example and hold yourself accountable to the actions of others
Never condone, commit, excuse, or remain silent about domestic violence. Check out The White Ribbon Campaign today.

Trade War
The war consists of several different interrelated battles

First Battle: Solar Panels and Washing Machines
- In October 2017, the United States International Trade Commission (USITC) warned that due to the increasing import of washing machines and solar panels from abroad, US domestic manufacturers were seriously injured (China is the central supplier).
- As a result, Trump imposes tariffs on the import of panels and washing machines (customs value: $10.5 billion)
- In response—China is investigating import of sorghum (Dura grain) from the US
- China imposes a 178.6 percent tariff on sorghum imported from the United States
- China cancels tariff after negotiations with the United States

Second Battle: Steel and Aluminum
- In April 2017, President Trump ordered an investigation into whether imports of steel and aluminum caused the US to be dependent on external sources leading to a threat to US national security
- The results of the investigation show that there is a threat to US national security
- As a result, Trump imposes tariffs on imports of steel (25%) and aluminum (10%) from anywhere in the world
- The European Union threatens to react
- Trump grants temporary exemption to Canada and Mexico following NAFTA negotiations
- Trump also grants temporary exemptions to the EU, South Korea, Brazil, Argentina and Australia
- China retaliates—imposing tariffs on imports from the United States of aluminum, pork, fruit and various products from the United States
- Trump cancels the temporary granted exemption to Canada, Mexico and the European Union

Third Battle: Technology and Intellectual Property
- In August 2017, the US began to investigate whether China was carrying out activities that infringed the intellectual property rights
- The results of the investigation show that China indeed violated these rights
- As a result, Trump imposes 25% tariff on imports of $55 technological and electronic products
- In response—China threatens to impose 25% tariff on cars, planes and soybeans imported from the United States
- In response—China imposes tariffs on a series of products from China (the date is not yet known)
- The United States announces that it intends to restrict the granting of visas to Chinese citizens
- The US is releasing an updated list of products that will be subjected to new tariffs starting in July
- In response—China publishes an updated list, as well
- The US is actualizing its threat—imposing tariff on products from China valued at $34 billion
- China is actualizing its threat—imposing tariffs on US products worth $500 billion

Fourth Battle: Aiming at High-Tech Companies
- There is no doubt that the battle is related to the previous one—technology and intellectual property. As the trade war worries, the Chinese
- The US prohibits Chinese company ZTE Corporation from acquiring US technologies after violating previous agreements prohibiting it from trading with Iran and North Korea
- The US starts to investigate whether Huawei violated the sanctions on Iran
- China is considering whether to allow US company Qualcomm to acquire NXP
- The US has imposed a fine of $1.3 billion on ZTE
- China is considering ratifying the Qualcomm—NXP deal
- The US has announced that it will lift the ban imposed on ZTE, after the company paid the fine and replaced its management
- Reports say that China expects to approve the Qualcomm-NXP deal
- The Chinese prohibit Micron from selling certain chips to China
- The Chinese prevent Qualcomm from acquiring NXP-the deal is canceled

Fifth Battle: Automotive Industry
- In May 2018, the US launched another investigation into whether the importation of cars into the United States creates dependence on manufacturers from abroad, posing a threat to national security
- Trump has reached an agreement with the president of the European commission to reduce tariffs on vehicle parts imported from Europe. In return, Europe will buy more soybeans from the United States and help it oppose China.
- The US Department of Commerce is scheduled to hold public hearings and investigate the issue of car imports to the United States

CORN STARCH

Only popcorn kernels can pop. Each kernel contains a small amount of water stored inside the soft starch casing.

When heated to around 450°F, the moisture turns to steam, creating pressure within.

The casing gives way and the kernel explodes, releasing steam and turning the kernel inside out. Now the popcorn is ready to eat.

A-MAIZE-ING FACTS

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in Central America, where the Olmec and Maya domesticated the grain and spread it to other cultures.

Corn was spread throughout the Western Hemisphere by European explorers and later introduced to Africa, Asia and Europe.

The American “Corn Belt” is the area of the United States that has the highest yields and most production of corn.

The Corn Palace in Mitchell, South Dakota, is a famous example of corn art and culture.

Corn’s health benefits include a rich source of vitamins and minerals, and its versatility in cooking and manufacturing.

The map section shows the Corn Belt region across the United States with major corn-producing states labeled.

Sources: Betty Fussell’s "The Story of Corn"; other references on corn production and history.

Healthy Futures - 20 Years and Counting

The year was 1992. Disney's Aladdin was the top movie of the year. Space Shuttle Endeavor completed its maiden voyage. In a lab at the University of Arizona, CBR was born — and with it, the entire family cord blood banking industry.

A History of Firsts:
1992: The 1st family cord blood sample is stored at CBR's Scientific Director's Laboratory at University of Arizona
1993: CBR Scientific Director releases the 1st banked sample to transplant
1996: CBR launches the 1st program to offer free banking services to families with medical need
1998: CBR is the 1st cord blood bank accredited by the American Association of Blood Banks
2004: CBR releases the 1st unit in the world to be used by a child for a brain injury
2005: CBR builds CBR
2010: CBR is the 1st to offer cord tissue banking to families in the U.S.
2010: CBR collaborates with Georgia Health Sciences University to initiate the first FDA-regulated clinical trial for cerebral palsy using a child's own cord blood stem cells
2012: 425,000+ samples banked - and counting

CBR Today:
"We're the largest, safest located, most experienced stem cell bank in the world — and we've got the numbers to prove it."
- 425,000+ Newborn stem cell samples banked, more than any other family bank in the world
- 38,569 Healthcare providers around the world
- 3,744 Hospitals that have used CBR's collection kit, including 753 international hospitals
- 100+ Countries store cord blood in CBR's bank
- 50 States are represented in CBR's bank

Fueling Awareness, Advancing Science:
- 25,000 Transplants worldwide
- 200+ Clinical trials underway
- 60 Percentage of total stem cells released for regenerative medicine applications, all within the last 8 years
- 4,000+ Samples CBR has banked free of charge
- 3,000 Number of physicians who have collected thanks to their Newborn Possibilities Program

The City Inside CBR:
It may be cold and dark inside CBR's stem cell storage tanks called Dewars, but there's a population of cord blood units safely tucked away — and thriving — equal in size to a large city
- -196°C Internal temperature
- Less than 24 hours Average time from sample's arrival at the lab to freeze

Dewars' population:
- 10,465 sets of twins
- 500 sets of triplets
- 12 sets of quads
- 1 set of quints

Some Things Don't Change
Number of chances to collect and store cord blood stem cells for your baby: 1

Coronavirus Disease 2019

BE AWARE.

COVID-19 is a new respiratory illness that was first discovered in Wuhan, China. It is transmitted from person to person.

COMMON SYMPTOMS:
- RUNNY NOSE
- COUGH
- SORE THROAT

SEVERE CASES:
- HIGH FEVER
- PNEUMONIA
- SEVERE RESPIRATORY DISEASE

*Symptoms may appear 1 to 12 days following exposure to the virus

Who is most at risk of becoming seriously ill?
- People over age 60
- People with pre-existing conditions such as diabetes and heart disease

How is it transmitted?
- Through close contact with an infected person
- By an infected person coughing or sneezing
- By touching contaminated objects or surfaces and then touching your mouth, nose or eyes

Currently there is no vaccine or specific treatment. We can only treat the symptoms.
Serious cases may require oxygen and ventilatory support.

PREPARE.
- Make sure you get your information from a reliable source.
- Wash your hands regularly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based gel.
- Cover your mouth with the inside of your elbow when you cough or sneeze or use a tissue and dispose of used tissue immediately and wash your hands.

ACT.
- Avoid close contact with people who have flu-like symptoms.
- Avoid touching your eyes, nose or mouth with unwashed hands.
- Avoid sharing cups, plates or other personal items and disinfect all surfaces that are touched frequently.
- If you have traveled to areas where COVID-19 is circulating or have been in contact with someone who has it and you experience fever, cough or difficulty breathing, seek medical attention immediately. Do not self-medicate.

Note: Information may change when more is known about the disease.
February 2020.

BE AWARE. PREPARE. ACT.

THE INFINITY STONES

POWER STONE
- Amplifies the user's strength, energy and destructive capability
- Can be used to obliterate planets and power weapons
- Seen in Guardians of the Galaxy (as an orb)
- Currently: In Thanos' gauntlet (seeking)

SPACE STONE
- Allows teleportation and instantaneous travel across space
- Housed in the Tesseract
- Seen in: Captain America: The First Avenger, The Avengers, Thor: Ragnarok
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

REALITY STONE
- Alters reality itself; can transform matter
- Also called the Aether
- Seen in: Thor: The Dark World
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

SOUL STONE
- Connects to the essence of a being; can manipulate souls and life and death
- Highly mysterious; special rules
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

TIME STONE
- Manipulates time: loops, fast-forwards, rewinds, traps
- Housed in the Eye of Agamotto
- Seen in: Doctor Strange
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

MIND STONE
- Controls minds and can grant sentience
- Housed in Loki's scepter, later embedded in Vision's forehead
- Seen in: The Avengers, Avengers: Age of Ultron, Captain America: Civil War
- Currently: Sought by Thanos

THE "FALSE" GAUNTLET
- A prop gauntlet went on display that isn't the final screen-used version
- It was much larger and more ornate than the gauntlet in the film
- This prop was used for promotional displays and photo-ops
- The real gauntlet used on set is more compact and designed to match Thanos' proportions

UNIVERSES & HOLLYWOOD

MARVEL CINEMATIC UNIVERSE
- The MCU is the most successful shared cinematic universe
- Launched by Marvel Studios in 2008 with Iron Man
- Films interconnect and characters cross over into ensemble movies such as The Avengers
- Marvel retained film rights to many characters but shared some rights with other studios; this has shaped who appears where
- Marvel Studios controls production of MCU movies while distribution and certain character rights involve other studios in some cases

DC EXTENDED UNIVERSE
- DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. created a shared universe for DC Comics characters beginning in the 2010s
- Includes films like Man of Steel, Batman v Superman, Wonder Woman, Justice League
- DC's approach often has a darker tone compared to the MCU

20TH CENTURY FOX
- Fox held film rights to the X-Men and Fantastic Four for many years, producing their own separate film series
- Because of this, those characters did not appear in the MCU films produced by Marvel Studios

SONY PICTURES
- Sony Pictures owns the film rights to Spider-Man and related characters
- A deal with Marvel Studios allowed Spider-Man to appear in the MCU while Sony retains distribution of standalone Spider-Man films

There are THREe phases of the MCU so far

Universes & Hollywood

Marvel Cinematic Universe
The idea of a shared universe was brought in by Marvel Studios after they saw the success of the Avengers comics. Marvel Studios started the MCU in 2008 with Iron Man and have slowly expanded the universe with other interconnected films. Key movies introduced the Infinity Stones which are central to Avengers: Infinity War.

DC Extended Universe
DC Entertainment and Warner Bros. Pictures created their own shared cinematic universe starting with Man of Steel. Their films are loosely connected, often darker in tone, and the shared universe has been built with different directorial visions.

20th Century Fox
Although they announced plans to bring their characters into a cohesive universe, the X-Men and Fantastic Four properties at 20th Century Fox largely remained in separate continuities for a long time. The Fox movies often existed independently of Marvel Studios' continuity.

Sony Pictures
Sony still controls the film rights to Spider-Man and certain related characters. A deal with Marvel Studios allowed Spider-Man to appear in the MCU while Sony retains distribution rights to standalone Spider-Man films.

"The 'false' gauntlet"
A replica glove used in the movie to cover Thanos' hand during filming. The stones were added in post-production. The real gauntlet used for motion capture differs slightly from the final product seen on screen.

The War on Grad School

An educated population is necessary for a country to remain competitive in the global marketplace, but often personal and societal decisions make attending grad school less desirable.

Who's Going to Grad School?

In fall 2014,
1.8 MILLION graduate students were enrolled in master’s, graduate certificate, or doctoral programs in the U.S.

There’s no doubt an education is necessary, yet when it comes to grad school, for some the cons outweigh the pros.

WHO’S FIGHTING THE WAR ON GRAD SCHOOL? // POLITICAL REFORMS

Tax Reform Act of 1986
Payments for teaching and research assistantships were made taxable.

Technical and Miscellaneous Revenue Act of 1988
Technical and miscellaneous revenue act changes removed the tax-free treatment of many graduate student benefits.

College Cost Reduction and Access Act of 2007
Decrease in interest rates for undergraduate loans
No change in interest rates for grad students

Budget Control Act of 2011
Graduate students lost eligibility for some subsidized loan benefits and saw cuts to research funding.

Bipartisan Student Loan Certainty Act of 2013
Graduate students saw a hike in interest rates tied to the market.

2016 Tax Reform
Originally proposed taxing the income of graduate students who receive tuition waivers as compensation.
Proposed changes gained attention but were not enacted into law in final tax reform package.

ARE THE CRITICS RIGHT?

Reasons not to pursue a graduate education include concerns about debt, opportunity cost, and personal sacrifices.

Social Media Platform Cheat Sheet

Pinterest
Key differentiating trait:
More of a niche platform, despite very large usage. Great for businesses that involve planning, design, creation, making. Heavily female audience.
Best for: Link clicks
Resource and expertise level needed: High
Good for these business types: Restaurants, Interior design, Wedding venues

Twitter
Key differentiating trait:
Fast-moving, real-time, news-driven platform. Strong customer service element, be prepared to engage and respond to customers here.
Best for: Link clicks, engagement, and foot traffic
Resource and expertise level needed: Low
Good for these business types: Retail, Nonprofits, Newspapers & media outlets

LinkedIn
Key differentiating trait:
Unlike almost every other platform, which is designed for the user to focus on their personal interests, this is a profession-driven platform.
Best for: Link clicks, event registrations
Resource and expertise level needed: Medium
Good for these business types: Luxury goods & services, Software providers, B2B companies

Instagram
Key differentiating trait:
There are very few ways to link from Instagram, so this is a visual branding platform. You need to have lots of high-quality images and videos to support this platform.
Best for: Foot traffic, word-of-mouth awareness, engagement
Resource and expertise level needed: Medium to high
Good for these business types: Bakeries & Coffee Shops, Travel agencies, Art museums

Facebook
Key differentiating trait:
This is the platform, but it doesn't mean you need to be there. With the recent challenges the platform has faced, some users are choosing to spend their social media time elsewhere (like Instagram, which is also a Facebook platform). That being said, there's still no better place to find and engage customers, donors, or others.
Best for: Link clicks, word-of-mouth awareness, event registrations
Resource and expertise level needed: Low
Good for these business types: Local Brick & Mortar Stores, Nonprofits, B2B Companies

Throughout the centuries, corn has been the object
of worship, prosperity, discovery and celebration.

Corn originates in
Central America, where
the Olmec and Maya
cultivated the tall grain

Early European explorers
were enlightened about the
New World grain in the 15th
and 16th centuries, and later
introduced it to other parts
of the world because of
its ability to grow in
diverse climates.

The American
"Corn Belt," the area of
land with the highest yield
of corn, stretches from North
Dakota to Ohio. It is such
a way of life in the American
Midwest that even the
University of Nebraska's
mascot is called
"The Cornhusker."

Corn's health
benefits include a rich
source of vitamins and
nutrients, including folic
acid, niacin and vitamin C.
High in fiber, it is also
believed to reduce the risk
of colorectal cancer,
hemorrhoids and heart
disease.

The Corn Palace in
Mitchell, South Dakota, was
originally built to showcase
the state's rich soil and
encourage people to settle
the area. It's now a popular
tourist destination decorated
with crop art murals and
designs made from real corn
and other grains.

COLOR

Profiles & Printing Explained!

What's the Difference?
CMYK, RGB, and Pantone are industry standards for color profiles. How do they compare and what are their uses? Find out now!

CMYK

CMYK is the standard color profile for printing. Typically, your printer uses CYAN, MAGENTA, YELLOW, and BLACK inks to print colored images. When all colors are mixed together, a rich black is produced.

CMYK USE

CMYK is used for printed materials. CMYK colors do not appear as bright as RGB (they are missing the added bonus of a light source). Brochures, business cards, posters, etc. may be printed in CMYK.

RGB

RGB refers to the three primary colors, RED, GREEN and BLUE. RGB colors use light to make their colors bright. If you mix them all together you would get pure white.

RGB USE

RGB is the color profile used for digital screens and devices. Your screen mixes red, green, and blue light to produce the bright colors you see on your display. Websites, mobile apps, video, etc. are in RGB.

PANTONE

The Pantone Matching System (PMS) is used to correctly match materials with their corresponding RGB or CMYK colors. This insures that a color looks the same each time it is produced when matched to a Pantone swatch.

PANTONE®
247 C

PMS USE

Pantone Matching System can be used to accurately match colors for anything from fabrics and paints to t-shirts and printed materials.

What About Printing?
Knowing the differences between the color profiles can help you to understand how different printing processes work and how those processes will affect your completed project.

SPOT COLOR (OFFSET)

+ =

PANTONE®
369 C

PANTONE®
350 C

Spot color printing describes the process where specialized ink or specially blended ink is created to make a perfect match that corresponds to your desired color, such as a Pantone swatch.

If there is just one color to be printed, there will be a single plate, and a single run of the press. If there are two colors, there will be two plates and two runs, and so on. The colors are layered onto the paper one by one.

BEST FOR:
Spot color printing would be typically used for jobs which require no full color imagery, such as for business cards and other stationery, or in monotone (or duotone etc) literature such as black and white newspaper print.

If you want something printed that has a unique color, like a metallic or fluorescent, spot color printing will be needed.

FOUR COLOR (OFFSET)

+ + + =

C M Y K

4 Color process printing refers to the CMYK printing process. This means your artwork will be printed using only Cyan, Magenta, Yellow, and Black ink colors.

Just like spot color printing, each color is run through one at a time. The layering of each ink color produces a final product with a full color spectrum.

BEST FOR:
Four color process is the most common form of printing and is best for large batches, like 500 postcards. It produces clear, sharp prints.

* In some cases it may be possible to do a six-color or eight-color print. This type of process is more expensive, but will produce a wider range of colors.

DIGITAL PRINTING

Digital printing uses either toner or ink-jet technology. Digital printing might still use CMYK, but all colors are applied at once instead of with the "offset" roller method.

Although it has been considered in the past to not be as "crisp" as offset printing, technologies continue to improve and digital printing can produce some great results.

BEST FOR:
Digital printing is recommended for small batches, like 20 invitations or just one poster.

Because digital printing does not require the creation of printing plates, turn-around time is often much faster.

businessofanalyticsweekly.com | info@businessofanalyticsweekly.com | www.analyticsweek.com

Investigating
Data Scientists

Understanding their Skills, Roles and Team Makeup

A Study of 600+ Data Scientists*

Respondents provided self-assessment ratings about proficiency in 5 skill areas:

1. Business
2. Technology
3. Programming
4. Math
5. Statistics

Respondents provided information about their team makeup, job role, industry, satisfaction with work outcome, gender and more.

Most respondents were from B2B companies (80%), North America (64%). About 46% work for companies with less than 100 employees.

*Results shown are from respondents who answered the relevant question. Respondents were recruited using AnalyticsWeek newsletter, May 2015. Respondents could select more than one industry; therefore totals may exceed 100%.

Top 5 Industries Employing Data Scientists

1. Information Technology (26%)
2. Education / Science (14%)
3. Consulting (13%)
4. Financial Services (11%)
5. Healthcare & Medical (9%)

[bar chart of industries — labels include many industries such as Information Technology, Education/Science, Consulting, Financial Services, Healthcare & Medical, Government, Retail, Telecommunications, Energy, Manufacturing, Media/Entertainment, Transportation, Other]

Data Scientists are Highly Educated

Most data scientists hold graduate degrees (~67%)

Nearly half of data scientists hold a Master's degree: 46%

There was no difference between males and females in their educational attainment

[stacked bar chart showing Education levels by gender — Graduate degree, Master's, Bachelor's, High School / Other]

Data Science Skills

Top 10 Data Science Skills

1. Communication
2. Structured Data
3. Data Mining and Visualization Tools
4. Science / Scientific Method
5. Math
6. Project Management
7. Data Management
8. Statistics and Statistical Modeling
9. Product Design and Development
10. Business Development

[horizontal proficiency bars showing distribution of proficiency levels from No Proficiency to Expert for each skill]

Proficiency Levels Legend:
No Proficiency | Basic | Intermediate | Advanced | Expert

Skill Areas of Data Science

1. Subject Matter
- Business domain expertise
- Product knowledge
- Strategy & goals
- Research questions & interpretation

2. Tools & Technology
- Programming, Data Storage, Middleware, Tools
- Data engineering, ETL, productionization
- Visualization, dashboards, BI, reporting

3. Statistics
- Descriptive & inferential statistics
- Hypothesis testing, experimental design, causal inference
- Machine learning, predictive modeling, time series
- Statistical thinking & modeling

4. Data Management
- Data integration, data cleaning, data quality
- Metadata, governance, security and access

[diagram combining these skill areas]

Data Science Roles

Four Types of Data Scientists

"Researcher" is the most popular data science job role

[bar chart showing distribution of roles: Researcher, Developer, Creative, Business Manager]

Descriptions:
- Business Managers include leaders, business persons and entrepreneurs
- Developers include developers and engineers
- Creatives includes Jack of all trades, artists and hackers
- Researchers include scientists and statisticians

Data Scientists are not Created Equal

- Business Managers understand business (i.e., subject matter) but not the stats
- Developers understand technology and programming but not business and stats
- Creatives possess a basic knowledge in analytics
- Researchers are strong in statistics and math but weak in business and programming

[radar chart illustrating strengths of each role across Subject Matter, Technology, and Statistics]

Data Science is a Team Sport

Diverse Data Science Teams Perform Better

Researchers and Developers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Business experts

[bar chart comparing project outcomes with and without Business experts for Researchers and Developers]

Business Managers report better outcomes of analytics projects when they work with Statistics experts

[bar chart comparing project outcomes with and without Statistics experts for Business Managers]

Bigger Teams, Bigger Outcomes

Data scientists who work with 4 or more people on their analytics projects report better outcomes compared to their counterparts who work with fewer people.

[bar chart comparing outcomes for teams of 1–3 people vs. 4+ people across Success metrics such as Project Success, Faster insights, Better adoption]

Getting Insights from your Data

The Scientific Method

1. Formulate a question or problem statement.
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable.
3. Gather/Generate data through experimentation or observation.
4. Analyze data/Draw conclusions (e.g., test hypothesis).
5. Communicate results/Take action (e.g., change processes) based on conclusions. Refine hypotheses for further tests.

[flow diagram showing steps and feedback loop]

The scientific method is a way of investigating phenomena, acquiring new knowledge, or correcting and integrating previous knowledge.

Three Primary Skills of Data Scientists

Data scientists bring three primary skills to bear on problems.

[three-circle Venn diagram: Subject Matter, Technology, Statistics — overlap labeled Data Science]

1. Subject Matter expertise helps you know what questions you need to ask.
2. Technology expertise helps you access the data and process it quickly.
3. Statistics expertise helps you apply the right analytics to answer your questions.

How to Apply Data Science Skills to the Scientific Method

The scientific method helps data scientists organize their collective efforts to extract insight and value from data.

1. Formulate a question or problem statement — Subject Matter
2. Generate a hypothesis that is testable — Subject Matter + Statistics
3. Gather/Generate data — Technology
4. Analyze data/Draw conclusions — Statistics + Technology
5. Communicate results/Take action — Subject Matter + Technology

[matrix diagram mapping each step to the three skill areas]

Learn More about the Practice of Data Science

For more detailed information about the results contained in this infographic, check out these articles:

- Getting Insights Using Data Science Skills and the Scientific Method http://bit.ly/1LQf0aZ
- Top Data Science Skills Report http://bit.ly/1LQf0aa
- Data Science Career Paths and the Practice of Data Science http://bit.ly/1LQf0ab
- Why Data Science Teams Report Better Work Outcomes http://bit.ly/1LQf0ac

Top 10 Skills Report (additional resources): http://bit.ly/Top10DataSkills
Report Methodology and Details: http://bit.ly/DataScienceReportMethod

Indonesia: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Sanitation Marketing, Household Subsidies
Peru: GDP Growth, Poverty Decline, Private Sector Interest, Policy Supporting Public-Private Partnerships

KEY CHALLENGES:
- Unmet Customer Needs
- Weak Supply Chain
- Difficult Investment Climate

- Show consumers that improved sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that match consumer preferences, needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

- Build business capacity and professionalism
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

- Consider alternative risk profiles that make investment attractive
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

MARKET DRIVERS

WHAT ARE CONSTRAINTS TO INVESTMENT?

UNMET CUSTOMER NEEDS
Why should I pay for a toilet or latrine?
Why should I settle if I can’t afford the dream?
The poor are challenging customers who would rather make do with their current sanitation situation if they cannot find a desirable, affordable product in their local market.

WEAK SUPPLY CHAIN
How do I reach poor households?
Why should I prioritize sanitation in my portfolio?
The sanitation market is highly fragmented and uncoordinated, making it difficult to develop and deliver products that consumers are willing and able to buy. Enterprises with the scale to tackle these industry challenges do not currently see sanitation or the poor as priority markets; businesses that do focus on sanitation have limited reach and product differentiation.

DIFFICULT INVESTMENT CLIMATE
How can we improve the ease of doing business in the sanitation sector?
Why should we invest in this even riskier business?
Current sanitation policies are not focused on lifting barriers to market entry. Despite the existence of government sanitation promotion programs, sanitation enterprises do not have enough information about viable markets and lack sufficient access to finance.

KEY CHALLENGES

RECOMMENDATIONS

1 Stimulate Demand
- Show consumers that good sanitation is a smart economic decision
- Design sanitation solutions that meet poor customers’ needs and preferences
- Offer flexible financing options

2 Strengthen the Sanitation Supply Chain
- Build business capacity and market intelligence
- Encourage formation of strategic partnerships to develop stronger distribution channels to reach remote customers

3 Improve the Investment Climate
- Communicate clear policies that promote improved sanitation
- Facilitate private sector provision by clearly defining the role of the private sector in government strategy and policy

Timeline entries:
Oct. 7, 2001: The first armed Predator drones begin flying missions over Afghanistan less than a month after 9/11. Predator missions in support of bombing close air support for troops expand.

Nov. 3, 2002: A CIA-operated Predator drone launched a Hellfire missile at a car carrying five Al Qaeda members. It was the first drone assassination in what would become a thousands-strong campaign.

Dec. 23, 2002: A Predator drone fires a sidewinder missile at an Iraqi fighter plane in the No-fly Zone. This is the first instance of air-to-air combat with a drone. During the surge in 2007, drone surveillance came together with networks of roadside bomb attacks.

May 7, 2004: The CIA begins a secret drone bombing campaign in Pakistan's tribal regions. Over this next five years, President George Bush would order 45 drone strikes within all five regions. From 2009 to 2011, President Barack Obama has ordered at least 160 strikes. Some estimates put the death toll from the campaign at close to 2,000.

Late 2004: The U.S. border Patrol begins testing UAVs for patrolling the U.S.-Mexico border. In 2005, it buys the first two Predator drones of its eventual squadron of regional surveillance aircraft.

Late 2009: U.S. begins drone flights off Somalia to monitor and strike pirates. In June 2011, the U.S. begins drone strikes at Al Qaeda-linked sites and launches drone strikes from within Somalia against al Qaeda and Shabab militants linked to al Qaeda.

April 21, 2011: First confirmed U.S. drone airstrikes rise up of drones in support of the Libyan rebels. The drones initially backfill air defenses in advance of the NATO bombing campaign, and provide targeting and support for NATO forces along the way. Operations in Libya mark the first use of armed U.S. Marine Corps drones.

Jan. 29, 2012: A Texas Department of Public Safety SWAT team deploys a small drone during a standoff with an armed suspect. This marks the first use of UAVs for domestic operations.

Notes: Data reflect capabilities at time of publication; numbers shown are as listed in the infographic.

Oct. 7, 2001: The first armed Predator drones began flying missions over Afghanistan less than a month after 9/11. By 2002, drones would begin flying close to the ground for troops on the ground.

Nov. 3, 2002: A CIA-operated Predator drone launched a Hellfire missile at a car carrying five al-Qaeda members. It was the first drone assassination in what would become a decades-long campaign.

Dec. 23, 2002: A Predator drone fires a sidewinder missile at an Iraqi fighter plane in the No Fly Zone. This is the first instance of air-to-air combat. During the Iraq war, drone surveillance would significantly reduce the number of roadside bomb attacks.

May 7, 2004: The U.S. Border Patrol begins testing UAVs for patrolling the U.S./Mexico border. In 2009, the U.S. begins flying drones inside Mexican airspace in support of Mexican government anti-crime efforts.

Late 2009: The U.S. begins drone flights over Somalia to monitor and deter pirates. In June 2011, the U.S. begins basing drones at the Mogadishu airport and launching drone strikes within Somalia against al‑Shabab militants.

April 21, 2011: President Obama approves the use of drones in support of the Libyan rebels. The drones destroy Qaddafi's armor in advance of NATO bombing operations and provide targeting and support to NATO allies during the war. Operations in Libya also feature the first use of drone helicopters in combat.

Jan. 25, 2011: A Texas Department of Public Safety SWAT Team deploys a small drone during a standoff with a suspect armed with a rifle. This marks the first use of unmanned aircraft for domestic surveillance.

question: What percentage of respondents said that their children has been engaged in physical activities daily or almost every day during the Covid-19 pandemic according to the survey?

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
The impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on Canadian families and children

We asked Canadians what it is like to be a parent during the COVID-19 pandemic.
Here are some of the participants' responses:

Screen time, reading and physical activity were the most popular daily activities
Daily or almost every day:
- Screen time: 89%
- Reading books or stories: 62%
- Physical activities: 61%
- Games: 23%
- Music, drama or visual arts: 21%
- Developing other skills: 18%

Percentage of participants who said that their children engaged in selected activities at home

Parents' top concerns were children's opportunities to socialize with friends and balancing child care, schooling and work

Percentage of participants who were very or extremely concerned for their families in terms of:
- Balancing child care, schooling and work: 74%
- Managing their child's or children's behaviour, stress levels, anxiety and emotions: 61%
- Having less patience, raising their voice, or scolding or yelling at their children: 46%
- Staying connected with family or friends: 43%
- Getting along and supporting each other: 37%
- Feeling lonely in their own home: 30%

Percentage of participants who were very or extremely concerned about their children's:
- Opportunities to socialize with friends: 71%
- Amount of screen time: 64%
- Loneliness or isolation: 54%
- General mental health: 48%
- School year and academic success: 40%

Parents of young school-aged children were the most likely to be very or extremely concerned about balancing child care, schooling and work
- Preschool-aged children only: 70%
- School-aged children aged 4 to 11: 80%
- School-aged children aged 12 to 14: 55%
- Children of various ages: 78%

For more information, please check out the Daily article.
Source: Statistics Canada, Impacts of COVID-19 on Canadians: Parenting During the Pandemic, June 2020.
© Her Majesty the Queen in Right of Canada as represented by the Minister of Industry, 2020

www.statcan.gc.ca

question: How many audio media types have been in use since the 1980s according to the infographic?

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
THE LIFESPAN OF STORAGE MEDIA

For as long we have been inventing ways to capture and present data, we have been developing media on which to store it. With each new innovation, we've seen greater convenience, increased storage capacity, and longer lifespans. But at some point, even with the utmost care, media fails... leaving us with a useless piece of plastic, tape, or metal.

The Cloud, with its extensive network of server farms, leverages redundant storage media, ensuring that in the event of hardware failure, the data lives on, stored safely in another part of the cloud. By way of comparison, we've assembled some lifespan statistics for storage media throughout the digital ages.

FAST | REDUNDANT | RELIABLE | ACCESSIBLE ANYWHERE | FOREVER

[Timeline showing years of use for various storage media types]

COMPUTER MEDIA:
- ZIP DISK (1994): 2-10 years
- 5" FLOPPY (1976): 2-30 years
- 3.5" FLOPPY DISK (1982): 2-15 years
- CD-RW (1997): 3-100 years
- DIGITAL TAPE (1987): 10-30 years
- USB FLASH DRIVE (2000): 10-75 years
- DVD-R (1999): 20-100 years
- HARD DRIVE (1956): 34-100 years
- SOLID STATE HARD DRIVE (1999): 51-100+ years

AUDIO MEDIA:
- COMPACT DISC (1982): 3-100 years
- REEL AUDIO TAPE (1950s): 10-20 years
- CASSETTE TAPE (1963): 10-20 years
- EIGHT TRACK (1964): 10-30 years
- MINI DISC (1992): 15-50 years
- VINYL RECORD (1881-1890s): 50-100 years

VIDEO MEDIA:
- VHS TAPE (1971): 5-15 years
- DV TAPE (1995): 10-20 years
- DVD (1995): 30-100 years
- SUPER 8 FILM (1965): 70-100 years

PHOTO MEDIA:
- UNDEVELOPED FILM (1889): 3-6 years
- DIGITAL PHOTOS (1990s): 30-50 years
- PHOTO SLIDES (1961): 50-70 years
- DEVELOPED PHOTOS (1900s): 50-75 years
- MEMORY CARD (1999): 115 years

ADDITIONAL WAYS MEDIA CAN FAIL:
- COMPATIBILITY ISSUES
- OXIDATION/CHEMICAL BREAKDOWN
- DRY OUT
- PHYSICAL DAMAGE

Notable callouts:
- CLICK OF DEATH: When the read/write heads of a hard disk would suddenly click. That signified that the disk was no longer usable and all data is lost.
- LOST IN SPACE: USB drives are often lost long before they ever fail.
- HEAD CRASH: When the media over writes into the MFM cylinder and crashes the drive.
- BE KIND REWIND: VHS tapes would often tear or break due to improper rewinding.
- COMPATIBILITY: 8-Tracks became an odd format when players disappeared.
- DJ SCRATCH N SNIFF: DJs intentionally damaged their decks and their vinyl destroying records in the name of learning how to DJ.
- VIDEO "HITS": Dropping digital tapes will cause dropouts during playback.
- COLOR FADES: B&W Photos will fade less than color photos. The dyes in colors are less stable.
- GRANDMASTER FLASH: Serial fails in mid and end bits from time.

THE BRUTAL ORIGINS

-- The tradition of Christmas is highly regarded as a celebration of the birth of Jesus Christ. Although popular belief states that Jesus was born on December 25th, The New Testament gives no specific date or year of his birth.

-- Roman pagans first introduced the holiday of "Saturnalia" which was a week-long period of lawlessness celebrated between December 17 - 25. During this period, Roman courts were closed, and Roman authorities stated that no one could be punished for breaking many of Rome's common laws.

-- The festival began when Roman authorities chose a random victim to represent "The Lord of Misrule." At the end of the festival on December 25th, the innocent man or woman would be brutally murdered. By doing so, Romans believed they were destroying the "forces of darkness."

-- In the 4th century CE, Christianity adopted the "Saturnalia" festival in attempts to convert large amounts of people to Christianity. The problem was - there was nothing intrinsically Christian about the festival. To fix this, Christian leaders claimed Saturnalia's final day, December 25th, was the official birth date of Jesus Christ.

...and Christmas was born.

Essay
War Literature

The Art of War

Author
Sun Tzu

Years Written
c. 475–221 BCE

Original Language
Chinese

CONTEXT

A Battle Won Before It Is Fought

Likely written during the violent and chaotic Warring States period of Chinese history (475–221 BCE), The Art of War offers a series of military strategies to wage war, involving such tactics as careful planning before battle. The strategies and tactics are analyzed and elaborated on by 13 commentators.

The Tao
Sun Tzu was influenced by Taoist philosophy, which emphasizes balance, virtuous choices, and harmony with nature.

Warring States Period
Sun Tzu lived during a turbulent period of military conflict among China's provinces.

Chinese War Literature
The Art of War was the first of the seven military classics in Chinese literature.

Key Figures

Li Ch'uan
T'ang dynasty writer of military strategy; prominent commentator

Ts'o Ts'o
Ruthless and effective King of Wei; senior commentator

Tu Mu
High-ranking government administrator; important commentator

Mei Yao-ch'en
Song dynasty poet and writer; major commentator and historian

The Art of War by the Numbers

3:1
Desired ratio of troops to commanding officers according to Sun Tzu

254
Years during the Chinese Warring States period

1772
Year The Art of War became available to the West when it was translated and published by a French Jesuit priest

24
Members of a U.S. spy plane crew freed from detention in China in 2001 after officials used The Art of War to come up with a diplomatic strategy

Main Ideas

Predecided Outcomes
Good resources, training, and strategy are the keys to victory.

Changing Tactics
War is unpredictable, a fact that good leaders use to their advantage.

Strengths & Weaknesses
Wise commanders know theirs and their enemies' strengths and weaknesses.

Deceit as Strategy
Fooling the enemy into making miscalculations wins battles.

A victorious army wins its victories before seeking battle.
Sun Tzu, Chapter 4

Author

SUN TZU
c. 400–301 BCE

An experienced professional tactician and strategist, Sun Tzu fought for the state of Wu during a time when China's states fought one another for primacy. He formulated his 13 topics of military strategy as an organized method of conducting war with the best possible outcome.

Sources: Deciphering Sun Tzu: How to Read The Art of War by Derek M. C. Yuen, Encyclopaedia Britannica, History.com

THE HISTORY
of social media

Social media channels seem to be everywhere and with 135 minutes of our time spent on social media platforms every day, it's crazy to think that it's only been 20 years since the first social media site was made. Without any further ado, here we have the history of social media.

THE BEGINNING
Samuel Morse made the first step towards some resemblance of social media when he sent a telegraph from Washington D.C to Baltimore. Morse's first message read "What Have God Wrought?"

1844

1969

MILITARY'S FIRST SOCIAL MEDIA ATTEMPT
ARPAN (Advanced Research Projects Agency Network) wanted to connect Universities on a proto-internet. It worked great, if transmitting two letters was the goal. Unfortunately the letters "LO" weren't the complete "LOGIN" they had planned to send over.

START OF SOCIAL SITES
From 1997 to 2001, Six Degrees had a peak of about 1 million members who were able to create profiles and 'friend' each other.

1997
sixdegrees

1999

CREATIVITY AND STATUS BLOGS
LiveJournal was a platform where users could blog as a form of keeping friends updated with their lives. A format later used by Facebook for their 'status'.

A CHALLENGER EMERGES
Friendster, one of the first social media sites, is created and launched by Jonathan Abrams. Within the next year, they turned down $30 million dollars from Google, widely considered one of the biggest blunders of Silicon Valley.

2002
friendster

2003

LINK UP WITH BUSINESSES

AARP FOUNDATION

- Rural: 32%

question: How many food items listed promotes LDL?

extracted_text_claude_sonnet__claude_son:
YOUR HEALTH IN ONE DROP

Healthcare is confusing. It makes managing your health hard. With changing health plans, multiple & disconnected practitioners, and wanting to focus on prevention, we need to take responsibility for our own health.

70% of data needed for accurate diagnosis and health management is in your blood. This data is critical for wellness and prevention, not just treating disease.

GOOD NEWS: Your blood cells regenerate every 120 days, so you can quickly measure significant improvement from lifestyle and nutritional changes.

CARDIOVASCULAR HEALTH
For 150,000 people each year, the first symptom of heart disease is death.
Approximately 47% of the people who experience a heart attack will die from it.

Blood diagnostic testing can provide greater knowledge about the risks for heart disease and other chronic illnesses by giving better insight into the following areas:

CHOLESTEROL
Not all cholesterol is dangerous. Some types, like High Density Cholesterol, can actually help clear your blood vessels.

HDL ("GOOD") CHOLESTEROL
FOODS THAT PROMOTE PROTECTION:
- Oatmeal
- Fish
- Nuts

LDL ("BAD") CHOLESTEROL
ELEVATED BY:
- Cheese
- Fried Foods
- High Glycemic Foods

HOWEVER, WE NEED TO LOOK BEYOND HD/LDL LEVELS.
50% of heart attack patients have normal HDL and LDL levels. Measuring your LDL sub-particles such as Apo B and Lp(a) gives you a more accurate assessment of your risks.

TRIGLYCERIDES
A form of fat in the body linked with heart disease and diabetes.

ACQUIRED BECAUSE OF:
- Obesity
- Cigarette Smoking
- High-Carb Diet
- Alcohol

Apo-B, which is not part of a standard cholesterol test, is responsible for linking bad cholesterol to blood vessels, and high levels can be a better predictor of risk of heart attack or stroke.

INFLAMMATION
A major contributor to many chronic diseases such as heart disease, cancer, and dementia is inflammation. Recent studies have even shown that inflammation negatively impacts exercise capacity. This can be measured in your blood by the High-Sensitivity C-Reactive Protein (hs-CRP) marker.

ANTI-INFLAMMATORY FOODS:
- Salmon
- Blueberries
- Oranges
- Green Tea
- Extra Virgin Olive Oil
- Papaya
- Sweet Potatoes
- Broccoli

THYROID
More than 1 in 10 Americans have Thyroid Disease, commonly causing fatigue and weight gain. Most are undiagnosed.

Hypothyroidism, a condition in which the thyroid produces too little thyroid hormone, causing the body to burn energy slower, can:
- Cause weight gain and increase risk for heart disease
- Increase cold intolerance and fatigue
- Increase cholesterol levels due to inhibition of the body's ability to metabolize energy

VITAMIN D
Because we're not connected to nature like our ancestors, more than 75% of US adults have insufficient levels of Vitamin D. This has been associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular disease, cancer, and infection.

Taking fish oil also aides in Vitamin D production.
Everyone has a different optimal level of Vitamin D: testing gives insight into how you achieve yours.

SMART BLOOD TEST
Regular blood diagnostics can help you keep track of your overall health in a more meaningful way and, more importantly, identify potential health threats that standard testing may not find.

CONTROL OF DATA

Having a wellness team review your results and offer holistic guidelines on how to improve your health ensures you aren't just healthy today, but you are optimized for life.

Sources: THEAPTEST.COM | NIH.GOV | RIGHTDIAGNOSIS.COM | HEART.ORG | JOHNSHOPKINSHEALTHALERTS.COM | ADVAMED.ORG

WellnessFX™ Created by COLUMN FIVE

HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square      permanent ink      nibbed drawing pen      gouache paint      paper      sticky tape

tissues      a couple of pencils      old cloths or towels      water      a flat-edged paintbrush      chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach

tape your paper to the desk for extra stability

work on a slanted desk or a table if possible

give yourself plenty of room to work

don't keep your left elbow next to your paper. If you knock it over, you'll spoil your work

make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

draw your brush point horizontally across the nib or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

(drawings of lined paper with guide marks and letters "adgh" shown)

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a light line just above the bottom of the rule. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a great guide to help you get consistent letter widths. First, mark the nib width along your ruler. Then use this measurement to mark across the page in a row of short vertical lines. Continue in this way until you have a diagonal line of 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical x-height for lowercase letters is about 4-5 nib heights. Using your ruler and a pencil, measure 4-5 nib heights and draw a horizontal line across your paper - this is your x-height.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height line, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below and draw two more lines. These are your ascender and descender lines.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals in your calligraphy. It is a line between the ascender and x-height line.

WARNING
If you're drawing your nib holder with your pen, you will smudge your ink! To avoid smudges while you draw use clean disposable tissues. If you must rest your hand on the page, use your work glove or a shield to keep the ink from smudging. We recommend using a block chisel tip marker before using your nib pen until your pen gets more comfortable.

SOME LETTERS TO GET YOU STARTED
Hold your pen at a 30° angle for this alphabet

(a set of lowercase letter illustrations with stroke numbers and small guide notes; labels for some letters:)
draw the letter with a 30° angle
draw this letter with a 45° angle
draw this letter with a 60° angle
1 2 3 4 (stroke numbers shown next to each letter)

(letters shown:)
a c e
s i t
b d p
q g y
j u h
n m r
w v x
f k z l

BUSINESS

vs
DATA
SCIENCE

In the age of big data, parsing unwieldy amounts of information can lead to world-changing innovation.
To understand this data, companies are hiring a variety of specialists, including business analysts and data scientists.

Who Are They?

BUSINESS ANALYSTS
Research and extract valuable information from structured and unstructured sources to explain historical, current, and future business performance; determine the best analytical models and approaches to present and explain solutions to business users.

DATA SCIENTISTS
Design, develop, and deploy algorithms through statistical programming that support business decision-making tools; manage large amounts of data; create visualizations to aid in understanding.

What Education Do They Have?

The majority of business analysts come from a variety of backgrounds including business and humanities, while data scientists tend to come from computer science, mathematics, and technology backgrounds.

What Skills Do They Have?

Both business analysts and data scientists are experts in using data to inform decisions, but they apply their skills in different ways, using the same or similar tools. The skills listed below describe someone who has earned a master's degree in the field.

Decision-Making
Business Analysts
• Instigate change by transforming business insights from interdisciplinary data analysis into tangible resources for business decision-making.

Data Scientists
• Discover the opportunity in the datasets by mining data and writing machine learning algorithms to support decision-making processes.

Applied Problem Solving
Business Analysts
• Define business problems and translate statistical analysis into data-driven business intelligence that improves business performance.

Data Scientists
• Create the foundation of analysis by which an applied business problem can be solved.

Data Analysis
Business Analysts
• Research, interpret, and visualize raw data using predictive, prescriptive and descriptive analysis to make it useful and accessible for business users.

Data Scientists
• Manage and organize large sets of multivariate data using methods like linear discriminant analysis and multinomial regression selection.

Analytical Models
Business Analysts
• Understand, integrate, and prescribe solutions that use data modeling.

Data Scientists
• Experienced statistical programmers using languages like and tools such as SAS, SQL, R, SPSS, Python, and Knime.

Database Management
Business Analysts
• Define and align database requirements for various forms of data, coded and not coded, using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Data Scientists
• Design and structure databases using tools like Teradata, Oracle, and Hadoop.

Many companies are just beginning to realize that workplace design directly impacts employee performance, yet research shows that 3 in 4 U.S. workers are not in optimal workplace environments.

Enjoy leading, persuading, promoting and selling
Energetic, competitive and ambitious

Enjoy working with records, numbers or machines
Organized, orderly and precise

Enjoy working with tools and machines
Practical, mechanical and physical

Enjoy studying, solving problems and working with information
Analytical, intellectual and scientific

Enjoy creative activities such as art, writing and music
Expressive, nonconformist, original and independent

Enjoy activities that help people, such as teaching and healing
Friendly, supportive and trustworthy

Identify the ideal workplace.

The Open Office

This modern setup gathers employees in a large, open space. Without walls or cubicles, employees interact with each other frequently.

70% of U.S. workers go to offices with open floor plans.

Who should work here?
Helpers  Sellers  Doers

Allows privacy and ownership over workspace while still efficiently using square footage. (green check)
May decrease productivity due to constant noise and interruptions. (red x)

The Cellular Office

This traditional workspace is divided into individual offices, typically with doors for added privacy. Offices may be shared with more than one person.

76% of workers in cellular offices are very satisfied in their work environment.

Who should work here?
Organizers  Sellers  Artists

Offers more privacy, which is key for certain professions, while encouraging concentration. (green check)
Not the most efficient use of space. Closed doors result in less collaboration. (red x)

The Cubicle Setup

Between the open office design and the cellular office is the cubicle setup, which divides an open space into semi-private areas using medium to high walls.

90 ft² is the average cubicle size, half the size of a typical executive office.

Who should work here?
Thinkers  Organizers  Sellers

Allows privacy and ownership over workspace while still efficiently using square footage. (green check)
Decreases employee communication (e.g., cubicle offices), while noise levels and distractions remain high. (red x)

Telecommuting

Thanks to advances in communication technologies, this increasingly popular work setup allows employees to work remotely either full- or part-time.

50% of U.S. workers hold a job that allows some telework.

Who should work here?
Doers  Artists  Helpers

Saves thousands of dollars in overhead costs. Employees enjoy more flexibility, fewer interruptions and greater autonomy. (green check)
May result in isolation, lack of teamwork, and a loss of productivity, depending on the worker's home situation. (red x)

Coworking Space

Coworking spaces are shared office environments where remote workers can rent a workspace with access to printers, scanners and meeting space.

2x The number of coworking spaces worldwide doubles every year.

Artists  Sellers  Organizers

Comes with its share of distractions and membership fees can be expensive versus working from home. (red x)
