HOW DO CORONAVIRUS DIAGNOSTIC TESTS WORK?
(aka "Do I have / Did I have COVID-19?")

A. Molecular Testing
VIRUS-DERIVED
[Diagram showing: Nasal swab → SARS-CoV-2 → RNA → DNA]
• DNA derived from patient samples is repeatedly copied and amplified

[Graph showing DNA Copies vs Light with gene 1, gene 2, and control lines]
• Different colored dyes bind to genes for pieces of the virus
• Another dye detects genes from related virus families

Pros:
• Early detection
• Quick processing (hours)
• Highly selective and specific

Cons:
• Skilled personnel and equipment necessary
• Does not tell if virus is still active
• Cannot detect those who've been infected and later recovered

B. Serological Testing
BLOOD-DERIVED

[Timeline showing]:
• SARS-CoV-2 infects a patient
• After 2 days: Your spleen produces IgM, one of the first antibodies to attack coronavirus
• 9-11 days: More tailored antibodies called IgG are produced

As the patient's immune system kicks in, the antibodies are measured using a technique called ELISA:

[Graph showing Antibody Concentration vs Light]

Pros:
• Rapid test available at point-of-care
• Low cost
• Can be used to test large populations
• Reliably detects exposure

Cons:
• Cannot detect if patient is contagious or infection is still present
• Prone to false negatives if patient has deferred immune response

Credits: CDC, C&ENews, MedRxiv, Scientific American, Seegene, ThermoFisher, UniProt

© 2020. Society for Laboratory Automation and Screening (SLAS)
Come Transform Research

HOW TO CREATE
BEAUTIFUL
CALLIGRAPHY

YOU WILL NEED...

a ruler or set square
permanent ink
nibbed drawing pen
gouache paint
paper
sticky tape

tissues
a couple of pencils
old cloths or towels
water
a flat-edged paintbrush
chisel-tip black marker

SETTING UP

your tools should be within easy reach

tape your paper to the desk for extra stability

work on a slanted desk or a table if possible

give yourself plenty of room to work

don't keep your left shoulder tense or you'll get poor spacing
if you rest your arm on the desk you will spoil your work

make sure you're comfortable

PRACTICE MAKES PERFECT

tape two pencils together with sticky tape

hold your pencils at a 45° angle - the tips of your pencils are like the nib of your pen

keeping the pencils steady, start making some marks, try some zig-zags or curves

FILLING YOUR PEN

never dip your pen directly into the ink pot

tilt your brush, scoop a horizontal strip of ink or gouache paint across the pen's nib

you're ready to start some calligraphy!

BEFORE YOU BEGIN...

put on some old clothes - calligraphy can be messy!

make sure you're sitting comfortably - try not to slouch

cover your lap with some old towels or cloths - ink can stain your clothes

PREPARING YOUR PAPER

(drawn guides image with letters a d g h and ruler marks)

1 DRAW YOUR BASELINE
Find this line by aligning your ruler lengthways across the top of your paper. Then, with your pencil, draw a line along the bottom edge of the ruler. This is your baseline.

2 CREATE A NIB LADDER
A nib ladder is a good way to get the size and spacing of your letters right. Using your pen nib, mark off the nib width along the left-hand edge of your paper, then repeat these nib widths down the page to make a ladder. Count out 10 squares.

3 DRAW THE X-HEIGHT
A typical italic typeface is about 4-5 nib heights for the x-height. Use your ruler to draw a horizontal line 4-5 nibs above the baseline. This will be the height of most lowercase letters.

4 DRAW THE ASCENDER & DESCENDER
From your x-height, measure 4-5 nib heights above and below to draw the ascender and descender lines. These show how far letters like b, d or g should extend.

5 DRAW THE CAP-HEIGHT
Cap-height is the height of the capitals. It should be around the same height as the ascender line.

WARNING
If you're drawing with your nib ladder or your nib over a wet area, be careful not to drag your paper across wet ink. We recommend using a block chisel-tip marker to practice once you're comfortable.

- Resource and expertise level needed: Low

COMCAST BUSINESS
Proud Partner of Team USA

As Team USA is preparing for the Olympic Winter Games 2018, it is important to realize how The Games have changed over the years for Team USA. From how they trained for the Olympic Winter Games 1924 - the first Olympic Winter Games - to how we watched and shared Team USA's success - it's a whole new world, a world driven by technology.

Explore our online timeline. You'll find facts and comparisons on how Team USA and The Games have changed through the years and get a glimpse into the technology behind them.

Welcome to:
Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games

THEN & NOW

ENHANCED TRAINING WITH TECHNOLOGY FOR TEAM USA

1924

Early Days for Team USA at the Olympic Winter Games

Inability to monitor vitals or receive feedback during training

Could not learn from the practice runs, which made coaching a guessing game

Difficult to access medical history and past injuries slowed down recovery times

NOW

Technology Helps Prevent Injuries and Speeds Recovery for Team USA

Skin patches monitor heart rate, body temperature and sweat composition during training

Coaches learn from live feeds to a mobile app

Quick access to past medical records shortens recovery time

TEAM USA ATHLETES PRACTICE SMARTER, NOT HARDER

1936

First Alpine Skiing Event for Team USA

Trained the old-fashioned way — skiing down mountains

Warm weather locations at a disadvantage, couldn't train year-round

No feel for the actual course beforehand

NOW

Team USA Can Now Simulate Ski Runs

Immersive video technology Team USA skiers emulate any course

Athletes get to know the course without leaving the training facility

Immediate feedback allows for improvement

CHANGING THE WAYS WE WATCH TEAM USA

1960

Team USA's First Olympic Winter Games Broadcast on TV

13 hours of coverage over 11 days*

15-minute selection of taped highlights aired from 11:15-11:30PM

All coverage was in black-and-white on TV

NOW

Broadcast TV Not the Only Way to Watch Team USA

1,500+ hours of multiplatform coverage over 18 days

Livestream all events including Team USA at the opening and closing ceremonies

10.8 million hours of The Games consumed

AFTER 30 YEARS, TEAM USA RETURNS TO SOUTH KOREA

1988

A Look at Technology the First Time Team USA Competed in South Korea

Low quality CRT "picture tube" TVs with a max screen size of 27"

Personal computers had found their way into only about 15 percent of U.S. households

Cell phones offered 60 minutes of talk time and 8 hours of power before needing to be charged

NOW

New Technologies as Team USA Returns to South Korea

Flat screen 4K Ultra HD Smart TVs now available up to 110"

80% of U.S. households contain at least one desktop or laptop computer, 23% have 3 or more*

Today's smartphones allow you to watch video in high definition

SMARTPHONES ARE CHANGING THE WAYS WE FOLLOW TEAM USA

2008

A New Device

Only 9% of Americans reported owning smartphones in 2008

Nearly impossible to have coverage of The Games while on-the-go

Television was the only way to watch live Team USA events

NOW

In The Palm of Everyone's Hand

Now 77% of Americans own smartphones

Livestream any Team USA event from any mobile device

Gain extra insight into the Team USA athletes

GENETICALLY MODIFIED ORGANISMS

WHAT?
Genetically modified organisms (GMOs) are organisms in which the genetic material (DNA) has been altered in a way that does not occur naturally.

WHY?
GM foods are developed because the perceived advantage is meant to translate into a product with a lower price, greater benefit (durability or nutritional value) or both.

RISKS?
GMOs have not been proven safe. The long term consequences of GMOs on our health and environment have not been adequately investigated.

The potentially detrimental effect on beneficial insects or a faster induction of resistant insects.

The potential generation of new plant pathogens.

The potential detrimental consequences for plant biodiversity and wildlife, and a decreased use of the important practice of crop rotation in certain local situations.

The movement of herbicide resistance genes to other plants.

VISUAL HISTORY OF
Christmas Trees

The first Christmas tree was the centerpiece of holiday festivities in fifteenth century Latvia. Young men and women danced and sang around the tree before setting it on fire the last night of festivities. From those early traditions, to the first American tree in 1816, and into the present Christmas season, Christmas trees have been the focal point of holiday cheer. This timeline takes a look at some of the significant moments in the modern history of this Christmas icon.

Landmark trees
Decoration inventions
Christmas culture
Tree industry

1600
First decorated tree

1883
First artificial tree

Sears, Roebuck & Company began offering the first artificial Christmas trees – 33 limbs for $.50 and 55 limbs for $1.00.

"It's not good to cut down trees for mere decoration," says President Teddy Roosevelt in 1901, launching a century long debate on real vs. artificial trees.

1910

1910s Glass candleholders & tinsel trees

Small electric lights are already invented, but are too expensive and dangerous for trees.

1920

1923 National Christmas Tree

The same large evergreen has been decorated every year since 1923. In 2011, the tree fell in a windstorm and a new one was planted.

1920s Tree farms

Overharvesting leads to evergreen shortages, leading to...

The industry grows rapidly. By 2002, over 40 million trees are sold in the U.S. every Christmas.

1920s Feather trees

Imported from Germany, feather trees become popular in U.S. and range in size from 2 inches to 6 foot.

1930
Brush company manufactures first bristle trees.

1933 Rockefeller Center

The tallest Christmas tree ever at Rockefeller was a 30 foot spruce in 1999.

1940

1940s Tree flocking

The West Coast reinvents Christmas with flocking kits that can be applied with vacuum cleaners.

1946 Bubble lights

1955 Disneyland Main Street Tree

Sixty foot tall live tree until 2008, when they switched to an artificial one for environmental reasons.

1958
Aluminum trees are first non-green artificial trees.

1957 How The Grinch Stole Christmas

Two classics that remind everyone it's the spirit of giving, not a tree, that makes Christmas.

1965 A Charlie Brown Christmas

Charlie's negative portrayal of artificial trees causes dip in sales.

1966 White House Blue Room Tree

National Christmas Tree Association begins presenting the White House with a tree from the annual Grand Champion award-winning growers.

1970

1970-80s
Plastic trees lead to explosion of artificial trees as green alternative.

1980 Largest tree lit in Gubbio, Italy

8.5 kilometers of cable outlines a 650 meter tall tree.

1989 A Christmas Vacation

The Griswold Family's quest for the perfect tree is full of hilarious holiday disasters.

1990

1990s Fiber-optic trees

In 1992, 46% of homes used an artificial tree. The number jumped to 58% in 2004.

2000s War on Christmas

Politicians debate whether it's more politically correct to refer to Christmas trees as 'holiday' trees.

Meanwhile, sales continue to boom. Over 17.4 million artificial trees were sold in 2007.

2001 Lifelike polyethylene trees

New consumer technologies drive a new era of Christmas tree decorations.

2011

which platform is right for you?

FACEBOOK
PROS
• largest social network in the world
• popular posts that are liked & shared can reach thousands
• easy to get started, add links, calls to action and events

CONS
• algorithm changes can reduce reach overnight
• 40% of users don't 'like' any brand pages so paid promotion can be the only way to reach them
• posts from friends & family are prioritised so you need likes and shares to get seen

10.86% average number of page followers who see a post
73% of 30-49 year olds
1.71 billion users a month and most popular social network for over-50s

TWITTER
PROS
• useful for networking with bloggers and press, and finding content to share
• tweets are indexed by Google so can boost your SEO
• taking part in Twitter chats is a great way to meet other shop owners and get advice

CONS
• character limits restrict how much you can say
• it's very fast-moving so posts quickly get buried
• many users find it confusing and don't stick around

24.6% of all verified users are journalists
18% tweets with images get 18% more clicks
320 million users a month but 44% of accounts have never sent a tweet

INSTAGRAM
PROS
• visual platform suits designers and makers
• lots of creatives use Instagram so it's easy to find your 'tribe'
• using hashtags enables posts to reach beyond your own followers

CONS
• no clickable links in captions means you have to work hard to encourage click-throughs
• posts can get hidden in non-chronological timeline
• introduction of Instagram Stories appears to have reduced engagement on normal posts

58x more engagement per follower than Facebook
53% of users are aged 18-29
400 million users a month

PROS
• as part of a vast search engine, pins have a longer shelf life than tweets or Facebook posts
• average spend from referrals is twice as high as from Facebook
• pins link to their source & the average pin is repinned 10 times

CONS
• you need to pin frequently to be most effective
• Pinterest has a big US bias, with 55% of users in the United States
• less interaction with customers

15-30 the ideal number you need to pin a day for best results
85% of users are female
100 million users a month
